text: The gold(I) catalyzed sulfination of aryl and heteroaryl boronic acids is described. Gold catalysis is well established as a highly powerful and selective method for activating $\pi$ systems and has led to the discovery of many new complex molecule constructions. In contrast, here we describe, through a full understanding of mechanism achieved through stoichiometric experiments and the isolation of catalytic gold-aryl and gold-sulfinate species, a gold catalysis reaction based on the common organometallic motif of gold(I) heteroatom bonding, and which operates through a simple redox-neutral transmetalation mechanism involving the reaction of the B(OH) 2 Ar and Au(I)-alkoxide derived from reaction of Au(I) X (X = Cl, Br) with base, SO 2 insertion into the gold(I) metal to carbon bond and a subsequent catalyst turnover by re-transmetalation of the B(OH) 2 Ar species. The gold catalysis reaction was effected with a 1-10 mol % catalytic gold loading using potassium metabisulfite (KHSO 2 O 3 ) as an $SO_2$ source and was general to all boronic acids regardless of electronic character (hyperconjugative, inductive, resonance effects). Heteroaromatic compounds that are highly relevant to medicinal chemistry were also readily sulfinated, allowing access to the corresponding functionalized sulfinate ligands in good to excellent yields that are amenable to further chemical transformation. Importantly, the versatility of the sulfinate species was dramatically demonstrated by a very straightforward and in situ transformation of the sulfinate to sulfones and sulfonamides, precursors to two of the major functional groups that make up a large proportion of the successful drug leads from modern pharmaceutical campaigns.
